parts.answer: • Digital thermometer has higher resolution e.g. measures to 0.1 ̊C Or digital thermometer has no parallax error • Digital thermometer has a lower (percentage) uncertainty Accept attempts to calculate percentage uncertainty for both thermometers for MP1 (1) (1) • Suitable control variable e.g. volume of oil, mass of oil Do not accept temperature of oil/room (1) • Suitable method of control for the control variable identified e.g. check the volume in the measuring cylinder after each pour check the mass using a balance (1) • Rate of flow = volume/time (1) 1 • Starting timer after oil has been poured e.g. marking a start position, starting at 10 cm • Stopping timer before the funnel is empty e.g. stopping after a fixed volume or at a marker • Record the volume after a fixed time period • Repeat for the same temperature and calculate the mean time • Use of light gates and (electronic) timer to avoid (human) reaction time (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 1 9 Number Answer Mark

• Curved line of best fit (1) • Minimum p.d. read from their line on the graph (1) • Use of W = VQ with Q = 1.6×10 –19 C and value of V from (b)(i) • Value of W in the range of 2.5×10 –19 to 3.0×10 –19 (J) Example of calculation W = VQ W = 1.8 V × 1.6×10 –19 C W = 2.9×10 –19 J (1) (1)

• Use of c = fλ (with λ = 625 nm) • Use of E = hf • h = 6.5 × 10 –34 J s (Use of E = hc/λ scores MP1 and MP2) Example of calculation c = fλ 3.0×10 m s –1 = f × 625×10 –9 m f = 4.8×10 Hz E = hf 3.1×10 –19 J = h × 4.8×10 Hz h = 6.5×10 –34 J s (1) (1) (1)

• There would be an uncertainty in wavelength/frequency Or there would be a range of wavelengths/frequencies Or the LED emits different wavelengths/frequencies • If wavelength was longer, the calculated Planck constant would be larger Or if the frequency was lower, the calculated Planck constant would be larger • There would be an uncertainty in the calculated Planck constant Or there would be a range of possible values of the Planck constant MP2 - Accept converse arguments for shorter wavelength or higher frequency (1) (1) (1)

• Take measurements for additional p.d.s between 1.5 and 2.0V Or Take measurements for smaller increments in p.d. • This would allow for a more accurate line of best fit to be drawn Or to more accurately identify the p.d. where the line touches the x-axis Accept use of a datalogger for MP1 (1) (1) Total for question 2 12 Number Answer Mark

• Voltmeter connected in parallel to solar cell only • Ammeter connected in series with the solar cell and resistor MP1 - Accept voltmeter connected in parallel to the resistor only, if no other resistance components (e.g. variable resistor, bulb, etc) are added Additional cells added – Not MP2. (1) (1) • Same distance between lamp and solar cell • Keep angle of solar cell to light the same • Block background light Or control background light level Or avoid casting shadows on the solar cell (1) (1) (1)
• Suitable method to vary solar cell temperature e.g. immersion in a water bath, clamped a fixed distance from a hair dryer (must include a method, rather than just naming a heat source) (1)
• Use of P = VI • P = 0.12 W Example of calculation P = VI P = 2.74V × 45×10 –3 A P = 0.12 W (1) (1)

• Use of I = P / A • Use of efficiency = useful power output / total power input • Efficiency = 0.17 (17%) Allow ecf from 3(d) for useful power output For MP1 & 2 accept a calculation of power output per m and efficiency calculated using input of 200 W m –2 For MP3 there should be no unit given or correct conversion to % Example of calculation I = P / A P = 200 W m –2 × (60×10 –3 ) P = 0.72 W Efficiency = useful power output / total power input Efficiency = 0.12 W / 0.72 W = 0.17 (1) (1) (1) • Too few readings • Too small a range of temperatures • Inconsistent intervals in temperature readings • Inconsistent d.p. in current values (1) (1) (1) (1) • Calculate output power for each temperature • Plot graph of power (on the y-axis) against temperature (on the x- axis) • Calculate the gradient (which is the power change per 1 ̊C) OR • Calculate output power for each temperature • Plot graph of temperature (on the y-axis) against power (on the x- axis) • Calculate 1/gradient (which is the power change per 1 ̊C) (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 3 15 Number Answer Mark

• Increase the mass of the slotted masses until rubber starts to slide • Calculate friction/tension/weight using mg Or measure weight of slotted masses (with a newton meter) (1) (1) • Add 250 g to mass m Or add 0.25 kg to mass m Or add 2.45 N to the weight of m • Use W = mg (1) (1) • Labels axes with quantities and units • Sensible scales • Plotting • Line of best fit (1) (1) (2) (1)

• Calculates gradient using large triangle • Value of μ in the range of 0.54 to 0.59 • Value given to 2 or 3 s.f. with no unit Example of calculation Gradient = (5.6 N – 0.6 N) ÷ (10.0 N – 1.0 N) = 0.556 (1) (1) (1)

• To test tyres would provide enough friction/braking force Or to test tyres provide enough grip Or to test the rubber on wet/icy/cold/loose surface materials • So that the tyres can stop the car in a safe distance/time Or to prevent cars from skidding/sliding (1) (1) Total for question 4 14 y = 0.5556x + 0.1175 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 4.5 5.0 5.5 6.0 F / N N/ N N / N F / N 2.45 1.4 4.41 2.5 6.38 4.0 8.34 4.6 10.3 5.8 Pearson Education Limited.
